career_path.mentor: Ozzie Newsome / Eric DeCosta / Joe Hortiz

scouting_tree.primary_mentor: Ozzie Newsome (Ravens) — foundational scouting philosophy; Howie Roseman (Eagles) — roster construction and collaborative leadership; Ryan Poles (Bears) — draft execution and BPA discipline
scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Newsome tree (BPA-first, 'you can never have enough good corners,' patient FA approach, value-first draft board, character emphasis, collaborative war room). Eagles tree (aggressive roster building, willingness to invest heavily in OL/DL, trade capital accumulation). Bears/Poles tree (strict BPA in round 1, offensive skill position investment, willingness to take non-need BPA picks like Loveland at TE when Cole Kmet was already on roster).
scouting_tree.breakpoints: No known breakpoints yet — zero drafts as GM. His stated philosophy exactly mirrors the Newsome/Poles DNA: BPA, build through the trenches, draft/develop/retain. Any breakpoints will emerge in 2026 draft and beyond.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: Zero drafts as GM means lineage weight is near-maximum. Reduced from 0.90 because his stated philosophy and four years observing Poles' execution provide some additional independent signal. Will drop to 0.60 after 2026 draft and 0.40 after 2027.

front_office.primary_decision_maker: Ian Cunningham — but reports to Matt Ryan (President of Football), who holds final decision-making authority per Arthur Blank's organizational structure

key_influencers.name: Matt Ryan
key_influencers.title: President of Football
key_influencers.known_biases: Former franchise QB. Values pass protection, QB development, and smart football. Emphasized wanting 'people that work well together' and collaborative decision-making. Stated vision is to run the football and stop the run. Has 'final decision-making authority' over GM and HC per Blank.

key_influencers.name: Kevin Stefanski
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Offensive-minded coach from Kubiak/Shanahan coaching tree. Prioritizes wide-zone run scheme, play-action passing, physical OL play. Values versatile offensive linemen who can zone block. Will want players who fit his run-first scheme. Two-time NFL Coach of the Year. Brought Bill Callahan as OL coach, Tommy Rees as OC.

key_influencers.name: Jeff Ulbrich
key_influencers.title: Defensive Coordinator (retained)
key_influencers.known_biases: Runs multiple defense, mixing 3-4 and 4-3 looks. Emphasizes multiplicity in fronts and coverages. Uses heavy Cover-1 man concepts (28.6% with Jets, vs league average). Attack-style pass rush philosophy. Broke Falcons franchise sack record (57) in 2025. Wants versatile EDGE players and coverage-capable LBs.

key_influencers.name: Bob Quinn
key_influencers.title: Senior Advisor to Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Former Lions GM (2016-2020). Longtime Patriots personnel executive before that. New England scouting background may add another layer of Belichick-tree influence. Serves as go-between for front office and coaching staff.

front_office.power_dynamics: Three-tiered collaborative structure: Matt Ryan (President of Football) sets vision and has final authority, Cunningham (GM) runs personnel/scouting/draft, Stefanski (HC) runs coaching staff and scheme. Ryan explicitly stated he wants the GM and HC to 'find ways to get to common ground.' Cunningham reports directly to Ryan. This is Cunningham's first GM job and Ryan's first executive role — both are invested in collaboration.
front_office.owner_interference: medium — Arthur Blank has fired three HCs and two GMs since 2017, showing willingness to act. However, he restructured the organization specifically to insulate football ops through the President of Football role. Historically patient with coaching hires but results-oriented. Created the new President of Football structure to delegate day-to-day decisions.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Best player available. Build through the trenches. Draft, develop, and retain. 'You can't have enough draft picks.' 'Smart, tough, physical.'
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: Inferred from mentorship tree: Strong BPA lean in early rounds (Poles took Loveland at TE 10th overall despite having Cole Kmet; took Rome Odunze at WR 9th when EDGE was a bigger need; Newsome's Ravens consistently drafted BPA and accumulated corners regardless of roster). Cunningham learned from Newsome's 'best available player regardless of need' mantra and Poles' strict BPA-first approach. Expect Day 2 to shift toward need-filling, particularly with only 5 picks and no first-round selection in 2026.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Cannot yet assess mismatch between stated and actual behavior — zero drafts as GM. The strongest signal is Poles' track record with Cunningham alongside him: Poles' BPA discipline was genuine (Loveland over positional need in 2025, Odunze over EDGE in 2024). However, with only 5 picks and no R1 in 2026, pure BPA may yield to need-driven approach by necessity.

trade_behavior.trades_per_draft_average: N/A as GM; Bears under Poles averaged 3-4 trades per draft
trade_triggers: Desire to accumulate more draft picks — Cunningham explicitly stated at Combine that he wants more than the current 5 picks
Cunningham learned in Chicago 'to use every method as humanly possible in order to manufacture picks'
Potential player trades to recoup draft capital (Jessie Bates, Drake London mentioned as trade candidates by media)
Tier breaks on board where value falls to them
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Hoards picks aggressively. 'I love picks, man. You use those as currency.' Said he will never 'mortgage the future for the now.' In Chicago in 2022, Bears entered with 6 picks and ended with 11 through four trades. Cunningham told his staff this will be 'the last time we'll ever have five picks' going into a draft.

position_avoidance: RB in early rounds — Poles never drafted an RB higher than R4; Newsome rarely invested early RB capital after Jamal Lewis. Cunningham's stated 'build through the trenches' philosophy deprioritizes skill positions relative to OL/DL.
position_premium: OT and DL — 'build through the trenches' is the stated cornerstone. Ravens under Newsome drafted OL/DL in first 3 rounds almost every year from 2009-2018. Bears under Poles prioritized OT Darnell Wright at 10 in 2023. CB also premium per Newsome mantra 'you can never have enough good corners.'

EDGE.archetype_summary: Versatile, multiple-front EDGE who can stand up or put hand in dirt. Ulbrich's defense requires multiplicity — players must be scheme-diverse. Prefer length and explosiveness with pass-rush upside. The 2025 Falcons broke the franchise sack record with an attack-style defense that featured EDGE players used in multiple alignments. James Pearce Jr. (Tennessee, R1 2025, 6-5, 243, 4.41 40) led the team with 10.5 sacks as a rookie. Jalon Walker (Georgia, R1 2025) was drafted as a versatile LB/EDGE hybrid. The Bears under Poles valued EDGE rushers with length — Austin Booker (6-4, 245, Kansas) was drafted in R5 2024 as a developmental piece.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33 inches preferred based on Ravens/Bears tree emphasis on length for EDGE
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Explosive first step (vertical jump 34+, broad jump 118+). Speed-to-power conversion matters more than raw speed. 3-cone under 7.10 for change-of-direction ability in multiple front alignments.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.55 or faster preferred; Pearce ran 4.41; Booker ran 4.56. Will accept slower times (4.60-4.65) if power and length are elite.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Versatility and scheme flexibility — must be able to rush from multiple alignments in Ulbrich's multiple front
most_valued_traits: First-step explosion and get-off
Ability to play in both 3-4 and 4-3 alignments
Pass-rush upside and bend around the corner
Length and leverage at the point of attack
Motor and effort consistency
least_important_traits: Elite run defense — the 2025 Falcons defense struggled stopping the run (23rd in rush EPA) despite elite sack numbers, suggesting run defense is secondary to pass-rush upside
deal_breakers: One-dimensional players who can only rush from a single alignment
Stiff athletes who lack the hip flexibility to convert speed to power
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite first-step explosion and athleticism — Pearce's 4.41 speed at 243 lbs made him worth a 1st-round trade-up

college_production.production_floor: 5+ sacks in final college season for early-round picks based on Ravens/Bears lineage emphasis on production
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending production — Pearce went from 5 sacks as sophomore to 10 as junior. Booker had 6.5 sacks in 2023.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Ulbrich's defense values versatile defenders, so production in multiple roles (pass rush + coverage) is valued

evidence.picks_analyzed: 2 (Falcons 2025 under prior regime, but Ulbrich's defensive scheme continuity makes these relevant); plus Bears EDGE picks under Poles/Cunningham
examples: 2025 R1 — James Pearce Jr. (Tennessee): 6-5, 243, 4.41 40. Speed rusher with elite first step. 10.5 sacks as rookie.
2025 R1 — Jalon Walker (Georgia): 6-2, 245. Hybrid LB/EDGE, versatile blitzer and coverage player.
2024 R5 (Bears) — Austin Booker (Kansas): 6-4, 245, 4.56 40. Developmental edge with length, played 26.5% of snaps as rookie.

OT.archetype_summary: Athletic, technically sound offensive tackle who excels in zone-blocking schemes. Stefanski's offense is built on wide-zone runs and play-action, requiring tackles who can reach-block, seal defenders, and move laterally. Bill Callahan's presence as OL coach means high-IQ, disciplined linemen are prioritized. The Bears under Poles/Cunningham drafted Darnell Wright (Tennessee, R1 10th overall 2023, 6-5, 333) — a versatile, powerful tackle who became a quality starter. Braxton Jones (5th round 2022) also became a multi-year starter, showing willingness to develop Day 3 OL into starters. The Bears drafted Ozzy Trapilo (Boston College, R2 56 2025, 6-6, 312) and Kiran Amegadjie (Yale, R3 75 2024, 6-5, 305) as developmental tackles.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 34 inches — standard OT threshold; Wright had 34.5-inch arms; Amegadjie had 33.75
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Lateral agility (short shuttle, 3-cone) most important for zone scheme. Must be able to move and reach in space. Poles emphasized 'lean mass' for OL — dense, athletic linemen.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: N/A for OT specifically, but athleticism in space is critical for zone-blocking

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Zone-scheme fit — must excel at reach blocks, combo blocks, and climbing to second level in wide-zone run game
most_valued_traits: Lateral movement and foot quickness for zone blocking
Technical discipline and IQ — Poles called Trapilo 'dependable, disciplined, technician, very intelligent'
Anchor strength and ability to sustain blocks in pass protection
Versatility to play both sides
Toughness and physicality at the point of attack
least_important_traits: Pure power/mauling ability in isolation — zone scheme values movement over phone-booth dominance
deal_breakers: Stiff, non-athletic linemen who can't move laterally in zone scheme
Poor technique that can't be coached up by Callahan
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite athleticism combined with starting experience at a Power 5 program

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter preferred — Wright started 40 games at Tennessee; Trapilo started 34 at BC; Amegadjie was a late bloomer at Yale
college_production.production_trajectory: Values multi-year starters with ascending play, but willing to bet on athletic upside from smaller schools (Amegadjie from Yale R3)
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — understands that competition level matters; still drafted Amegadjie from Ivy League based on athletic traits

examples: 2023 R1.10 (Bears) — Darnell Wright (Tennessee): 6-5, 333, 34.5 arms. Zone-fit tackle, PFF 82.2 run-blocking grade in 2024.
2022 R5.168 (Bears) — Braxton Jones (Southern Utah): 6-5, 310. Day 3 developmental tackle who became 3-year starter.
2025 R2.56 (Bears) — Ozzy Trapilo (Boston College): 6-6, 312. Poles described as 'dense player that can get movement at POA.'
2024 R3.75 (Bears) — Kiran Amegadjie (Yale): 6-5, 305. Athletic upside pick from Ivy League, limited by injuries as rookie.

IOL.archetype_summary: Smart, athletic interior lineman who fits the zone-blocking scheme. Stefanski's offense and Callahan's coaching demand guards and centers who can pull, combo block, and reach at the second level. The Bears under Poles acquired quality IOL through FA (Joe Thuney, Drew Dalman) rather than early-round draft picks, but drafted Day 3 developmental pieces. The Falcons already have All-Pro Chris Lindstrom and Matthew Bergeron as IOL starters, so depth/development picks are more likely. Luke Newman (Michigan State, R6 2025) was a versatile IOL pick under Poles.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 32 inches for guards; 33+ preferred for centers
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Short shuttle and 3-cone for lateral movement in zone scheme. Bench press for functional strength. Must show ability to get to the second level.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: N/A but must demonstrate athletic ability to pull and reach-block

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Zone-blocking fit — ability to execute reach blocks, climb to linebackers, and sustain combos
most_valued_traits: Intelligence and communication at the line of scrimmage
Lateral agility and movement skills for zone concepts
Functional strength and ability to anchor vs. bull rush
Versatility to play multiple IOL positions
Consistency and reliability
least_important_traits: Pure power-scheme dominance — not looking for road-graders who can't move
deal_breakers: Poor athletic profile that doesn't fit zone scheme
Limited football IQ
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Center-capable versatility and elite zone-blocking traits

college_production.production_trajectory: Values consistency and durability over flashy traits

examples: 2025 R6.195 (Bears) — Luke Newman (Michigan State): 6-4, 305. Versatile IOL who can play guard and center.
Acquired via trade/FA (Bears) — Drew Dalman, Joe Thuney. Shows willingness to supplement draft IOL with veteran FA acquisitions.

DT.archetype_summary: Penetrating, disruptive interior defender who can generate interior pressure. Ulbrich's attack-style defense needs DTs who can collapse the pocket and disrupt run lanes. The Bears under Poles/Cunningham drafted Gervon Dexter Sr. (Florida, R2 53rd 2023, 6-3, 312) as a high-upside 3-technique, and Zacch Pickens (South Carolina, R3 2023, 6-4, 305) and Shemar Turner (Texas A&M, R2 62nd 2025, 6-4, 285) as additional interior pieces. The Falcons' 2025 defense saw rapid growth from second-year DTs Brandon Dorlus and Ruke Orhorhoro. ESPN's Jordan Reid identified nose tackle as 'a clear need for the Falcons' in his 2026 mock.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33 inches preferred for interior defenders to control blockers
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: First-step quickness (10-yard split) most important. Vertical jump and broad jump as explosiveness indicators. Poles looked at 'lean mass for the bigs.'
physical_profile.speed_requirement: N/A but quickness off the snap is critical

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Interior disruption — penetrate gaps and collapse the pocket from inside
most_valued_traits: First-step quickness and get-off
Ability to generate interior pressure as a pass rusher
Strength at the point of attack to hold ground vs. double teams
Versatility to play multiple interior alignments in multiple fronts
Motor and effort
least_important_traits: Pure two-gap space-eating — Ulbrich's attack style prioritizes penetration over holding space
deal_breakers: Lack of pass-rush ability — Ulbrich's defense is built on generating pressure from everywhere
Poor first-step quickness
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite interior pass-rush ability combined with position versatility

college_production.production_floor: 3+ TFLs in final college season for early-round DTs
college_production.production_trajectory: Values ascending production and development curve
college_production.context_sensitivity: Adjusts for scheme and role — credits interior players who generated pressure in limited pass-rush opportunities

examples: 2023 R2.53 (Bears) — Gervon Dexter Sr. (Florida): 6-3, 312. High-upside 3-technique who became starting DT in year 2.
2023 R3 (Bears) — Zacch Pickens (South Carolina): 6-4, 305. Value pick, struggled to crack depth chart.
2025 R2.62 (Bears) — Shemar Turner (Texas A&M): 6-4, 285. Interior DL with pass-rush ability.

CB.archetype_summary: Physical, man-capable cornerback with length and ball skills. Ulbrich's defense ran heavy Cover-1 man concepts with the Jets (28.6% of snaps, 7th in NFL). This demands corners who can play press-man and mirror receivers one-on-one. Newsome's Ravens had the famous mantra 'you can never have enough good corners' and drafted CBs in the first 3 rounds in 6 of 8 years. The Bears under Poles/Cunningham drafted Kyler Gordon (Washington, R2 39th 2022, 5-11, 194, 4.52) and Tyrique Stevenson (Miami, R2 56th 2023, 6-0, 198, 4.35) as their top CB investments. A.J. Terrell anchors the Falcons secondary on a long-term deal through 2028.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 31 inches — length matters for press disruption in Ulbrich's man-heavy scheme
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash is critical — Stevenson ran 4.35, Gordon ran 4.52 (outlier — more of a zone/slot type). Short shuttle for hip fluidity. Vertical jump for ball skills.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster strongly preferred for outside corners in man coverage; slot corners can be slightly slower with better short-area quickness

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Man-heavy coverage with Cover-1 emphasis per Ulbrich's scheme; must be able to press and mirror
most_valued_traits: Man coverage ability and hip fluidity
Press technique and physicality at the line
Ball skills and play-on-ball ability — Falcons defense had 16 INTs in 2025 (6th in NFL)
Tackling willingness in run support
Competitive toughness
least_important_traits: Pure zone instincts — less critical given Ulbrich's man-heavy tendencies, though multiplicity means some zone ability is useful
deal_breakers: Inability to play man coverage
Lack of competitive toughness or avoidance of physical play
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite man-coverage skills combined with plus ball skills and physical play

college_production.production_floor: No strict statistical floor — Newsome/DeCosta Ravens valued film over interception totals for CBs
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers multi-year starters, but willing to develop — Gordon had one elite season at Washington; Stevenson had two strong years at Miami
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — values quality of competition and role in coverage scheme over raw PBU/INT numbers

examples: 2022 R2.39 (Bears) — Kyler Gordon (Washington): 5-11, 194, 4.52 40. Versatile CB who became quality starter, more zone/slot profile.
2023 R2.56 (Bears) — Tyrique Stevenson (Miami): 6-0, 198, 4.35 40. Physical press-man corner with elite speed.
2025 R5.169 (Bears) — Zah Frazier (UTSA): Developmental DB pick on Day 3.

S.archetype_summary: Rangy, ball-hawking safety who can play multiple roles in the secondary. Ulbrich's defense values safeties who can play single-high, split-field, and come down into the box. The Falcons' 2025 draft class included Xavier Watts (Notre Dame, R3) and Billy Bowman Jr. (Oklahoma, R4), both of whom became instant contributors — Watts had 5 INTs as a rookie. The Bears under Poles/Cunningham drafted Jaquan Brisker (Penn State, R2 48th 2022, 6-1, 199, 4.49) as a hybrid safety with range and physicality.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash under 4.55 for range. Vertical jump 36+ for ball skills and closing speed. Short shuttle for change of direction in zone coverage.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.55 or faster preferred; Brisker ran 4.49; Watts was a 4.46 type

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Versatility — must be able to play single-high free safety, split-field coverage, and come down as a box defender
most_valued_traits: Range and ability to cover ground as a deep-half or single-high defender
Ball skills and ability to create turnovers — Watts/Bates combined for 8 INTs in 2025
Physicality in run support and willingness to tackle
Versatility to play multiple safety roles
Football IQ and communication ability
least_important_traits: Pure box safety/run-support-only profile — the defense needs coverage versatility more than a linebacker-sized hitter
deal_breakers: Limited range that restricts coverage assignments
Poor ball skills
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite ball production (INTs) combined with range and coverage versatility

college_production.production_floor: 2+ INTs in final college season for early-round safeties; Watts had 7 INTs at Notre Dame in 2023
college_production.production_trajectory: Values high ball production — consistent with Newsome's emphasis on turnovers for the defense
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderately high — weights ball production heavily regardless of competition level

examples: 2022 R2.48 (Bears) — Jaquan Brisker (Penn State): 6-1, 199, 4.49 40. Hybrid safety with range, physicality, and coverage ability.
2025 R3 (Falcons, prior regime but Ulbrich scheme) — Xavier Watts (Notre Dame): Ball-hawking FS, 5 INTs as rookie.
2025 R4 (Falcons, prior regime) — Billy Bowman Jr. (Oklahoma): Versatile DB who contributed immediately.

LB.archetype_summary: Coverage-capable, sideline-to-sideline linebacker who can operate in Ulbrich's multiple defensive scheme. Must be able to cover TEs and RBs in man and zone, blitz effectively, and tackle in space. Ulbrich's Jets defenses featured LBs in heavy coverage roles. Kaden Elliss (2025 All-Pro candidate, 30 pressures, 100+ tackles) exemplifies the ideal: instinctive, productive, coverage-capable. The Bears under Poles drafted Noah Sewell (R5 2023) and Ruben Hyppolite II (R4 2025, Maryland) as developmental LBs. Poles typically addresses LB later in the draft.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash under 4.60 for sideline-to-sideline range. 3-cone under 7.00 for change of direction in coverage. Short shuttle for mirror ability against TEs.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.60 or faster preferred for modern coverage LB

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Three-down ability — must be able to play all three downs without being a liability in coverage
most_valued_traits: Coverage ability against TEs and RBs in man and zone
Sideline-to-sideline speed and pursuit angles
Blitz ability and pass-rush effectiveness from LB depth
Tackling reliability in space
Football IQ and play recognition
least_important_traits: Pure run-stuffing thumper profile — modern game requires coverage ability above all for LBs
deal_breakers: Coverage liability who can be exploited by TEs and RBs in the passing game
Limited range and inability to cover ground sideline to sideline
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite coverage skills combined with blitz ability — the hybrid LB/EDGE profile (like Jalon Walker)

college_production.production_floor: 70+ tackles in final college season for Day 2 LBs
college_production.production_trajectory: Values multi-year starters with ascending production
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — values coverage stats (PBUs, INTs for LBs) as much as tackle numbers

examples: 2025 R1 (Falcons, prior regime) — Jalon Walker (Georgia): 6-2, 245. Elite hybrid LB/EDGE with coverage and blitz ability.
2023 R5 (Bears) — Noah Sewell (Oregon): 6-1, 251. Developmental LB with sideline-to-sideline range.
2025 R4 (Bears) — Ruben Hyppolite II (Maryland): Developmental LB, wasn't invited to Combine but showed effort traits.

WR.archetype_summary: Productive, physical, YAC-capable receiver who can operate in Stefanski's play-action-heavy offense. The Bears under Poles/Cunningham invested heavily in WR: Rome Odunze (Washington, R1 9th 2024, 6-3, 215) was a big-bodied, contested-catch X receiver with plus production (92 catches, 1640 yards). Luther Burden III (Missouri, R2 39th 2025, 6-0, 206) was an electric YAC weapon. Poles also drafted Velus Jones Jr. (R3 2022, 6-0, 204, 4.34) as a speed/special teams addition — a miss that shows willingness to swing on athletic traits. Stefanski's offense features play-action shots and needs reliable possession receivers who can win after the catch. Drake London is the Falcons' established WR1.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum — varies by role (outside vs slot)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: YAC ability is paramount — Poles described Burden as 'the best [run-after-catch] in this class.' Speed matters but is role-dependent. Contested-catch ability for outside WRs.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster preferred for outside receivers; 4.40 or faster for designated speed threats

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Production and versatility — must be able to win at multiple levels of the field and contribute in multiple ways
most_valued_traits: Run-after-catch ability and contact balance
Competitive toughness and contested-catch winning
Route-running ability and separation creation
Play-action integration — ability to attack soft spots created by run fakes
Versatility to play inside and outside
least_important_traits: Pure deep-speed specialist profile — Stefanski's offense is not a vertical-heavy system
deal_breakers: Drops and concentration issues
Inability to contribute after the catch
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite YAC ability and competitive toughness — Poles drafted Burden partially because 'the run-after-the-catch is special, probably the best in this class'

college_production.production_floor: 55+ catches or 800+ yards in final college season for Day 2 WRs; Odunze had 92/1640/13; Burden had 192 career catches
college_production.production_trajectory: Values consistent multi-year production over single-season breakouts
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — weights production highly but adjusts for offensive system and target share

examples: 2024 R1.9 (Bears) — Rome Odunze (Washington): 6-3, 215, 4.45 40. Big-bodied WR with elite college production (92/1640/13). Contested-catch specialist.
2025 R2.39 (Bears) — Luther Burden III (Missouri): 6-0, 206. Electric YAC weapon with 192 career catches. 'Best run-after-catch in this class.'
2022 R3.71 (Bears) — Velus Jones Jr. (Tennessee): 6-0, 204, 4.34 40. Speed/return specialist — a reach/miss that shows willingness to bet on athleticism.

TE.archetype_summary: Versatile receiving tight end who can also hold up as an in-line blocker. Stefanski loves multiple TE sets — he ran heavy 12-personnel in Cleveland and his offense regularly featured TEs as primary pass-catchers. The Bears under Poles/Cunningham drafted Colston Loveland (Michigan, R1 10th 2025, 6-5, 253) as a pure BPA pick despite already having Cole Kmet. Loveland led the Bears with 700+ receiving yards as a rookie. This was the signature BPA pick of the Poles/Cunningham era — taking a TE at 10 when it wasn't a pressing need. Kyle Pitts is being franchise-tagged by the Falcons for 2026.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed for a TE (4.60 or faster preferred). Vertical jump and broad jump for contested-catch and red-zone ability. Agility tests for route-running capability.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.65 or faster preferred for receiving TEs; Loveland ran approximately 4.60

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Receiving production from the TE position in Stefanski's heavy 12-personnel packages
most_valued_traits: Receiving ability and route-running from the TE position
Ability to create mismatches against LBs and safeties
Enough blocking ability to stay on the field in all situations
Red-zone production and contested-catch ability
YAC ability and ability to threaten the seam
least_important_traits: Elite blocking as primary skill — receiving TEs are prioritized in Stefanski's system
deal_breakers: Pure blocking TE with no receiving upside
Inability to separate from LBs in coverage
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite receiving ability at TE — Loveland was taken 10th overall as BPA even with Kmet already on roster

college_production.production_floor: 40+ catches or 500+ yards in final college season for early-round TEs
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — adjusts for TE usage rate in college scheme

examples: 2025 R1.10 (Bears) — Colston Loveland (Michigan): 6-5, 253, ~4.60 40. Pure BPA pick — led Bears with 700+ receiving yards as a rookie despite Cole Kmet already on roster.

RB.archetype_summary: Deprioritized in the draft. Both the Newsome/Ravens and Poles/Bears trees historically avoided early RB investment. The Bears under Poles never drafted an RB higher than R4 (Roschon Johnson, R4 115th 2023, Texas), with Trestan Ebner (R6 2022) as the only other drafted RB. Poles found his best RB production through UDFA Ian Wheeler and late-round pick Kyle Monangai (R7 2025, Rutgers). The Falcons already have Bijan Robinson, a top-3 NFL RB, so this position is extremely unlikely to be addressed early. Stefanski values a featured back in his wide-zone scheme, and Robinson is an elite fit.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: N/A — not a primary concern for RBs
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Burst and vision more important than top-end speed. Short shuttle for lateral movement in zone scheme. Pass-catching ability valued.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.55 or faster preferred but not a hard cutoff given zone-scheme fit

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Zone-scheme fit — must have patience, vision, and lateral agility to execute in wide-zone run concepts
most_valued_traits: Vision and patience to let zone blocks develop
Lateral agility and one-cut ability
Pass-catching ability and pass protection
Durability and ability to handle high-volume workload
least_important_traits: Top-end breakaway speed — Stefanski's run game is about efficiency and sustained drives, not home-run plays
deal_breakers: Inability to pass protect
Poor vision and decisiveness in zone-blocking scheme
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: This front office is extremely unlikely to reach for an RB given philosophical deprioritization and Robinson's presence

college_production.production_floor: No established floor — late-round/UDFA is the preferred investment level
college_production.production_trajectory: N/A — not a primary draft target
college_production.context_sensitivity: Low priority — will draft for depth/special teams only

examples: 2023 R4.115 (Bears) — Roschon Johnson (Texas): 6-0, 225. Short-yardage back, 502 yards and 8 TDs in two seasons.
2022 R6 (Bears) — Trestan Ebner (Baylor): Speed back, no longer in NFL.
2025 R7.233 (Bears) — Kyle Monangai (Rutgers): Late-round steal, contributed immediately.

QB.archetype_summary: Not an immediate draft priority. The Falcons have Michael Penix Jr. (2024 R1 pick, currently rehabbing ACL injury) and Kirk Cousins (contract situation unresolved). Stefanski's offense asks the QB to operate from under center, execute play-action, manage the game, and make accurate intermediate throws. Stefanski coached Kirk Cousins in Minnesota and Baker Mayfield/Joe Flacco/Deshaun Watson in Cleveland — he tailors the offense to QB strengths but prefers a pocket-forward passer with enough mobility to escape pressure. The Bears under Poles/Cunningham drafted Caleb Williams (USC, R1 1st overall 2024) — an elite dual-threat talent — but that was a special circumstance (generational prospect at 1.01).

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Arm strength and accuracy most important. Processing speed and decision-making under pressure. Enough mobility to extend plays.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: N/A — pocket awareness and play-extension ability more important than 40 time

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Game management, accuracy, and play-action execution in Stefanski's system
most_valued_traits: Accuracy on intermediate routes, especially off play-action
Processing speed and pre-snap reads
Pocket presence and ability to navigate pressure
Leadership and toughness
Enough mobility to extend plays — Stefanski has always catered the offense to fit the quarterback
least_important_traits: Elite rushing ability — Stefanski's offense doesn't require a running QB, though he adapted for Watson
deal_breakers: Poor accuracy on play-action shots — the bread and butter of Stefanski's offense
Inability to operate from under center
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite processing and accuracy — but only if franchise QB caliber; will not draft developmental backup high

college_production.production_floor: Must be a multi-year starter at a competitive program
college_production.production_trajectory: N/A — only relevant if franchise-caliber prospect available
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Stefanski and Matt Ryan both deeply understand QB evaluation

examples: 2024 R1.1 (Bears) — Caleb Williams (USC): 6-1, 218. Generational talent, Heisman winner. Rare arm talent, mobility, and processing. Poles said 'He's all ball, wants to work, wants to get better, wants to win as a team.'

QB.confidence_note: QB evaluation from Bears tenure was a singular generational prospect scenario. Not indicative of how this front office would evaluate mid-round or developmental QBs. Penix and Cousins' status will determine if QB is addressed at all.

reach_tolerance.notes: Inferred from Bears/Poles tenure with Cunningham: Poles was generally disciplined on value — traded back from 9 to 10 in 2023 to pick up an extra R4 pick while still getting Wright. Velus Jones Jr. at R3 71 in 2022 was perceived as a reach (WR who was 25 years old). Loveland at 10 was not a reach (universally seen as top-10 talent). Overall, the Poles/Cunningham Bears were value-disciplined in early rounds with occasional reaches on Day 2/3 for scheme-specific athletes.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: Shanahan/Kubiak-influenced wide-zone run scheme with play-action passing. Stefanski was influenced by Gary Kubiak in Minnesota. Offense features under-center formations, heavy use of pre-snap motion, wide-zone runs, and play-action deep shots. Bill Callahan as OL coach ensures zone-blocking fundamentals. Tommy Rees as OC with play-calling duties. Values athletic, versatile offensive linemen who can zone-block.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: Multiple front defense mixing 3-4 and 4-3 looks under Jeff Ulbrich. Heavy Cover-1 man coverage tendency (28.6% with Jets, 7th in NFL). Attack-style pass rush philosophy — Falcons had franchise-record 57 sacks in 2025. 14 different players recorded sacks. Uses nickel as base (~70% of snaps). Values versatile EDGE players and coverage-capable LBs.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: MAJOR scheme change on offense. Previous regime under Raheem Morris ran a different offensive system. Stefanski's wide-zone, play-action scheme is a complete install. Offensive player archetypes may shift significantly. Defense retains Ulbrich continuity from 2025 — no scheme change on that side.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderately conservative. Inferred from Newsome/Poles lineage: Newsome was historically risk-averse, preferring 'red star' players (smart, high-effort, high-character). Poles avoided Jalen Carter at 9 in 2023 partly due to character concerns, trading back one spot. However, Poles also took the BPA gamble on Loveland at 10 when it wasn't a need — showing willingness to take calculated risks. Cunningham likely falls in the conservative-to-moderate range.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Moderate. Cunningham inherits a team with specific recent failures (no playoffs in 8 years, poor run defense in 2025, QB uncertainty). The franchise's frustration may push toward addressing immediate weaknesses. However, Cunningham's stated BPA philosophy and Poles' mentorship should provide some insulation against pure recency reactions.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Unknown as GM. Poles showed willingness to move on from investments that weren't working (traded Roquan Smith mid-season, moved on from N'Keal Harry quickly). Cunningham likely follows this pattern given shared philosophy.
psychological_profile.combine_description: Balanced. Ravens lineage was film-first but valued the combine as a confirming tool. Newsome's draft process emphasized 'meticulous tape analysis and instinctual assessments.' Poles described wanting 'lean mass for the bigs' at the Combine, showing he uses measurables to filter but doesn't over-index on testing alone. Cunningham was described as emphasizing 'meticulous tape analysis' from his Ravens training.
psychological_profile.character_description: High character emphasis. Newsome's Ravens were known for avoiding character risks (he famously chose Ogden over Lawrence Phillips in 1996 despite owner pressure). Poles avoided Jalen Carter partly due to character concerns. Cunningham's introductory presser emphasized 'smart, tough, physical' — 'smart' being the leading descriptor suggests high character/intelligence standards. However, the 2025 Falcons draft under the prior regime included James Pearce Jr., who was subsequently arrested on felony charges — Cunningham inherits this situation but didn't make the pick.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Build around the franchise QB. Poles/Cunningham went all-in on Caleb Williams at 1.01 in 2024, then immediately surrounded him with weapons (Odunze, Loveland). In Atlanta, the QB situation is unclear — Penix is rehabbing ACL, Cousins' contract is unresolved. Stefanski has publicly been noncommittal. Cunningham is unlikely to draft a QB high with limited draft capital and existing investments, but Matt Ryan's presence as a former elite QB may shape how the organization evaluates the position.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Leans toward production but makes occasional projection bets. Odunze (92/1640/13) and Burden (192 career catches) were highly productive picks. Loveland was a productive college TE. However, Austin Booker (6.5 sacks in one year) and Amegadjie (Ivy League) were more projection-based. In tiebreakers, production likely wins — consistent with Newsome's value-first, disciplined approach.

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: 55+ catches or 800+ yards in final season for Day 2 picks — Odunze had 92/1640, Burden had 56/714 in 2024 (lower year in his 3-year arc)
production_thresholds_by_position.RB: No minimum — only drafted late rounds; production less relevant than scheme fit
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: 5+ sacks in final season for top picks — Pearce had 10 sacks at Tennessee
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: No clear statistical threshold — film-first for CBs per Ravens lineage
production_thresholds_by_position.TE: 40+ catches preferred — Loveland had 59 catches at Michigan in 2024
production_thresholds_by_position.OT: Multi-year starter at Power 5 or equivalent

college_production_preferences.notes: Ravens lineage emphasizes that production matters but context does too. Newsome's mantra was BPA — and production is a key input to determining who the 'best player' is. Cunningham's Eagles tenure under Roseman also valued production — Roseman drafted highly productive players like DeVonta Smith, Jalen Hurts (at a value slot), and Dallas Goedert.

WR.notes: Bears drafted WRs across the athletic spectrum — Odunze (4.45), Jones (4.34), Burden (4.45). Values production and YAC over pure speed.

DT.notes: First-step quickness valued over combine numbers. Poles looked at 'lean mass' over raw weight.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Mostly soft preferences rather than hard cutoffs. The Ravens/Poles tree uses measurables as confirming data alongside film rather than eliminators. Will break thresholds for exceptional film grades.

size_preferences_by_position.DT: 285+ for 3-tech, 310+ for nose — flexible based on production
size_preferences_by_position.LB: 225-250 range preferred for modern coverage LB
size_preferences_by_position.WR: No strong size preference — range from 5-10 to 6-3 in recent draft picks

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: Poles emphasized 'lean mass for the bigs' — values body composition and functional athleticism over raw testing numbers. Vertical jump and broad jump as explosiveness proxies. 3-cone and short shuttle for lateral movement (critical for zone-blocking OL and coverage defenders).
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Film-first overall. Cunningham's Ravens training was described as emphasizing 'meticulous tape analysis and instinctual assessments.' However, measurables serve as a floor/filter at premium positions. Poles publicly stated Burden was 'the most talented player on the board' based on film first, with athleticism confirming.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: N/A for 2026 — Falcons have no first-round pick (traded to Rams for James Pearce Jr. in 2025). Historical Bears/Poles R1 approach: strict BPA regardless of need. Took Loveland (TE) at 10 when it wasn't a pressing need. Took Odunze (WR) at 9 over EDGE when EDGE was a bigger need. This BPA discipline should carry to future drafts when Cunningham has a R1 pick.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: This is where the Falcons' 2026 draft effectively begins. Expect a shift toward need-informed BPA — Cunningham has limited capital (5 picks) and needs to maximize every selection. Poles' Bears shifted toward need-filling on Day 2 — Stevenson (CB, R2) and Dexter (DT, R2) were both need-meeting BPA picks. With picks at 48 and 79, Cunningham will likely target the best available player at a position of need, prioritizing DL (nose tackle), WR, IOL, or EDGE.
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Developmental athletes and scheme-specific fits. Poles consistently found Day 3 contributors: Braxton Jones (R5 LT starter), Roschon Johnson (R4 short-yardage RB), Kyle Monangai (R7 steal). Expect Cunningham to target high-upside athletes with limited college production, special teams contributors, and positional depth. With only picks at 114, 195, and 231, every Day 3 pick must have a clear path to contributing.
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: No comp picks in 2026 draft. Cunningham will need to manufacture additional picks through trades to supplement limited capital.

OL.note: Bears/Poles OL drafting was solid — Wright, Jones, Trapilo all starters. Amegadjie TBD. Cunningham's OL background (former guard) likely aids evaluation.

WR.note: Odunze and Burden were strong picks; Velus Jones was a miss.

CB.note: Gordon and Stevenson both became starters, though Stevenson had off-field issues.

DT.note: Dexter became starter but Pickens and Bell struggled. Interior DL evaluation is a potential blind spot.

S.note: Brisker was a quality pick. Small sample size.

EDGE.note: Booker (R5) was developmental only. Bears didn't invest high capital at EDGE in the draft — traded for Montez Sweat instead.

QB.note: Caleb Williams at 1.01 — generational talent, not indicative of mid-round QB evaluation.

TE.note: Loveland at 10 — signature BPA pick that paid off immediately.

blind_spots: Interior DL evaluation appears inconsistent — Dexter worked out but Pickens and Bell did not. May need to adjust confidence for Day 2/3 DT picks.
strengths: OL evaluation is a clear strength — consistent with Cunningham's playing background as a guard at Virginia. CB evaluation has been strong. WR evaluation improving after early miss on Jones.

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Moderate and strategic. Cunningham explicitly said he won't 'mortgage the future for the now' and wants to avoid dead money. This aligns with Newsome's Ravens, who were 'patient and deliberate' in FA, often waiting out the initial frenzy. Poles' Bears were FA-active when roster holes demanded it (Thuney, Dalman, Byard) but always maintained draft as the primary building method. Expect Cunningham to be strategic in FA — filling 1-2 critical holes while preserving cap flexibility.
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When a position is addressed in FA, it drops in draft priority but isn't eliminated. Poles signed Keenan Allen in FA but still drafted Odunze (WR, R1) and Burden (WR, R2) — showing that FA additions don't prevent BPA picks at the same position. However, with only 5 picks, the Falcons may be more strategic about not doubling up.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through the draft at premium positions, supplement through FA and trades. 'Draft, develop, and retain' is the stated core. Cunningham values draft capital as currency — can be used for picks or traded for proven players. Prefers to retain homegrown talent rather than overpay in FA.

draft_history_summary.total_picks_analyzed: 0 as Falcons GM; 40+ analyzed from Bears tenure (2022-2025) where Cunningham was AGM
notable_patterns: Bears under Poles/Cunningham had offense-heavy early-round drafting: no defensive player in R1 from 2019-2024 (longest streak in NFL)
Strict BPA approach in round 1 — took Loveland at 10 despite having Kmet, took Odunze at 9 despite EDGE need
Active traders — averaged 3-4 trades per draft, frequently moving back to accumulate picks
OL investment through both draft and FA — prioritized building the trenches
Day 3 is for developmental athletes and special teams — Braxton Jones (R5 → starter) and Kyle Monangai (R7 → contributor) were best values

biggest_reaches.context: 25-year-old speed receiver with limited production. Poles compared him to Deebo Samuel/Tyreek Hill versatility. Has been the most criticized pick of the Poles/Cunningham tenure.

biggest_steals.context: Small-school (Southern Utah) OT who became a 3-year starting left tackle.
7th-round pick who became one of the draft's best steals per multiple outlets.

confidence_notes: Zero drafts as Falcons GM. Profile is 85% lineage-based (Newsome → Roseman → Poles mentorship tree) and 15% based on Cunningham's own stated philosophy, which perfectly mirrors his mentors. Bears draft history (2022-2025) is the best proxy data since Cunningham was intimately involved as AGM — Poles credited Cunningham extensively and brought him to key events like Williams' Pro Day. Key risk factors: first-time GM may deviate from mentor patterns; new coaching staff (Stefanski) creates scheme-driven draft needs that may override pure BPA; no first-round pick limits ability to express R1 tendencies; Matt Ryan's influence as President of Football is an unknown variable. Profile should be significantly refreshed after the 2026 draft.
